meta.book: Esther (Addition C: Prayers of Mordecai and Esther)

preamble.summary: Addition C, inserted after Esther 4:17, provides the extended prayers of Mordecai (vv. 1-15) and Esther (vv. 16-30). These prayers fill the most glaring theological silence in the MT: the moment of crisis when the people face annihilation. Mordecai's prayer affirms God's sovereignty and explains his refusal to bow as theological rather than political. Esther's prayer is a deeply personal confession — she removes her royal garments, covers herself in ashes, and pleads as a vulnerable Israelite woman.
preamble.remarkable: Esther's prayer contains a stunning confession: she declares that she hates the bed of the uncircumcised and abhors her position as queen. She claims to have eaten nothing at Haman's table, drunk no wine of libations, and found no joy since her elevation — 'except in you, O Lord, God of Abraham.' Whether historically plausible or not, the prayer transforms Esther from a successful assimilationist into a suffering exile maintaining hidden faithfulness. The prayers together add over 100 explicit references to God in a book that originally contained none.
preamble.friction: These prayers have no Hebrew counterpart and dramatically change the book's theological character. The MT Esther achieves its effects through divine absence — God works through 'coincidence,' human courage, and political maneuvering. The LXX additions replace this with explicit divine intervention, which some scholars argue diminishes the MT's more sophisticated theology of hiddenness.
preamble.connections: Esther 4:15-17 (MT context); Daniel 9:4-19 (national confession prayer); Nehemiah 9 (historical review prayer); Judith 9 (woman's prayer before dangerous action); Addition D (Esther's approach to the king).

verses.text_source: Greek: Καὶ ἐδεήθη Μαρδοχαῖος τοῦ κυρίου μνημονεύων πάντα τὰ ἔργα κυρίου
verses.text_reference: Then Mordecai prayed to the Lord, calling to remembrance all the works of the Lord.
verses.rendering: Then Mordecai prayed to the Lord, calling to mind all the works of the Lord.
translator_notes: Mordecai's prayer fills the theological silence of the MT. Where the MT never names God, the LXX gives Mordecai an extended prayer that explicitly invokes God as Creator, Sovereign, and Redeemer.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ εἶπεν Κύριε κύριε βασιλεῦ πάντα κρατῶν
verses.text_reference: He said, 'O Lord, Lord, you rule as King over all things, for the universe is in your power.'
verses.rendering: And he said, 'O Lord, Lord, you who rule as King over all things, the universe is in your power,

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ οὐκ ἔστιν ὃς ἀντιτάξεται σοι
verses.text_reference: 'and there is no one who can oppose you when it is your will to save Israel.'
verses.rendering: and there is no one who can stand against you when you are determined to save Israel.'

verses.text_source: Greek: σὺ ἐποίησας τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν
verses.text_reference: 'For you made heaven and earth and every wonderful thing under heaven.'
verses.rendering: 'For you made heaven and earth and every wondrous thing under heaven.

verses.text_source: Greek: σὺ εἶ κύριος πάντων
verses.text_reference: 'You are Lord of all, and there is no one who can resist you, the Lord.'
verses.rendering: You are Lord of all, and there is no one who can resist you, O Lord.'

verses.text_source: Greek: σὺ πάντα γινώσκεις
verses.text_reference: 'You know all things; you know, O Lord, that it was not in insolence or pride or for any love of glory that I did this, and refused to bow down to this proud Haman.'
verses.rendering: 'You know all things. You know, O Lord, that it was not out of insolence or pride or desire for glory that I refused to bow to this arrogant Haman.
translator_notes: Mordecai's self-defense is crucial: his refusal was theological, not political. He would have been willing to humble himself for Israel's sake.

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι ηὐδόκουν φιλεῖν πέλματα ποδῶν αὐτοῦ πρὸς σωτηρίαν Ισραηλ
verses.text_reference: 'For I would have been willing to kiss the soles of his feet, for the salvation of Israel.'
verses.rendering: For I would have willingly kissed the soles of his feet for the salvation of Israel.'
translator_notes: This verse is remarkable: Mordecai claims he would have performed the most abject humiliation — kissing Haman's feet — if Israel's welfare required it. His refusal was not about personal dignity but about giving divine honor to a human.

verses.text_source: Greek: ἀλλ᾽ ἐποίησα τοῦτο ἵνα μὴ θῶ δόξαν ἀνθρώπου ὑπεράνω δόξης θεοῦ
verses.text_reference: 'But I did this so that I might not set human glory above the glory of God, and I will not bow down to anyone but you, my Lord.'
verses.rendering: 'But I did this so as not to place the glory of a mortal above the glory of God. And I will not bow to anyone except you, my Lord,

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ οὐ ποιήσω αὐτὰ ἐν ὑπερηφανίᾳ
verses.text_reference: 'and I will not do these things in arrogance.'
verses.rendering: and I do not do this out of arrogance.'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ νῦν κύριε ὁ θεὸς ὁ βασιλεύς ὁ θεὸς Αβρααμ
verses.text_reference: 'And now, O Lord God and King, God of Abraham, spare your people.'
verses.rendering: 'And now, O Lord God and King, God of Abraham — spare your people!

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι ἐπιβλέπουσιν ἡμῖν εἰς καταφθοράν
verses.text_reference: 'for our enemies look upon us with envy, and they have desired to destroy the inheritance that has been yours from the beginning.'
verses.rendering: For our enemies look upon us with envy and desire to destroy the inheritance that has been yours from the beginning.

verses.text_source: Greek: μὴ ὑπερίδῃς τὴν μερίδα σου ἣν ἐλυτρώσω ἑαυτῷ ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου
verses.text_reference: 'Do not neglect your portion, which you redeemed for yourself out of the land of Egypt.'
verses.rendering: Do not disregard your portion, which you redeemed for yourself from the land of Egypt.'

verses.text_source: Greek: εἰσάκουσον τῆς δεήσεώς μου
verses.text_reference: 'Hear my prayer, and have mercy upon your inheritance; turn our mourning into feasting, that we may live and sing praise to your name, O Lord.'
verses.rendering: 'Hear my prayer and have mercy on your inheritance. Turn our mourning into feasting, so that we may live and sing praise to your name, O Lord.'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ μὴ κλείσῃς στόματα τῶν αἰνούντων σε
verses.text_reference: 'Do not destroy the mouths of those who praise you.'
verses.rendering: 'Do not silence the lips of those who praise you.'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ πᾶς Ισραηλ ἐκέκραξεν ἐξ ἰσχύος αὐτῶν
verses.text_reference: And all Israel cried out mightily, for their death was before their eyes.
verses.rendering: And all Israel cried out with all their strength, for death was before their eyes.

verses.text_source: Greek: Καὶ Εσθηρ ἡ βασίλισσα κατέφυγεν ἐπὶ τὸν κύριον
verses.text_reference: Then Queen Esther, seized with deathly anxiety, fled to the Lord.
verses.rendering: Then Queen Esther, gripped by mortal anguish, fled to the Lord.
translator_notes: Esther's prayer is the longest addition and the most theologically rich. She removes her royal garments, covers herself with ashes, and prays as a humble Israelite — a dramatic transformation from queen to penitent.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἀφείλατο τὰ ἱμάτια τῆς δόξης αὐτῆς
verses.text_reference: She took off her splendid apparel and put on the garments of distress and mourning. Instead of costly perfumes she covered her head with ashes and dung.
verses.rendering: She removed her splendid garments and dressed in clothes of distress and mourning. In place of costly perfumes, she covered her head with ashes and dung.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐταπείνωσεν τὸ σῶμα αὐτῆς σφόδρα
verses.text_reference: She utterly humbled her body; every part that she loved to adorn she covered with her tangled hair.
verses.rendering: She humbled her body severely, and every part she had loved to adorn she covered with her tangled hair.

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ ἐδεήθη κυρίου θεοῦ Ισραηλ
verses.text_reference: She prayed to the Lord God of Israel, and said: 'O my Lord, you only are our King.'
verses.rendering: And she prayed to the Lord God of Israel and said: 'O my Lord, you alone are our King.

verses.text_source: Greek: βοήθησόν μοι τῇ μόνῃ
verses.text_reference: 'Help me, who am alone and have no helper but you.'
verses.rendering: Help me, for I am alone and have no helper except you.

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι κίνδυνός μου ἐν χειρί μου
verses.text_reference: 'for my danger is in my hand.'
verses.rendering: For my peril is close at hand.'

verses.text_source: Greek: ἐγὼ ἤκουον ἐκ γενετῆς μου ἐν φυλῇ πατριᾶς μου
verses.text_reference: 'Ever since I was born I have heard in the tribe of my family that you, O Lord, took Israel out of all the nations.'
verses.rendering: 'From my birth I have heard in the tribe of my family that you, O Lord, chose Israel from among all the nations,'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ τοὺς πατέρας ἡμῶν ἐκ πάντων τῶν προγόνων αὐτῶν
verses.text_reference: 'and our ancestors from among all their forebears, for an everlasting inheritance, and that you did for them all that you promised.'
verses.rendering: and our fathers from among all who came before them, as an everlasting inheritance, and you have fulfilled for them all that you promised.'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ νῦν ἡμάρτομεν ἐνώπιόν σου
verses.text_reference: 'And now we have sinned before you, and you have handed us over to our enemies.'
verses.rendering: 'And now we have sinned before you, and you have handed us over to our enemies,

verses.text_source: Greek: ὅτι ἐδοξάσαμεν τοὺς θεοὺς αὐτῶν
verses.text_reference: 'because we glorified their gods. You are righteous, O Lord!'
verses.rendering: because we glorified their gods. O Lord, you are righteous!'

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ νῦν οὐχ ἱκανώθησαν
verses.text_reference: 'And now they are not satisfied that we are in bitter slavery, but they have covenanted with their idols.'
verses.rendering: 'And now they are not satisfied with our bitter slavery, but they have made a covenant with their idols

verses.text_source: Greek: τοῦ ἐξᾶραι τὸ ὁρισθὲν τοῦ στόματός σου
verses.text_reference: 'to abolish what your mouth has ordained, and to destroy your inheritance, to stop the mouths of those who praise you.'
verses.rendering: to abolish the decree of your mouth, to destroy your inheritance, to silence the lips of those who praise you,

verses.text_source: Greek: καὶ σβέσαι δόξαν οἴκου σου καὶ θυσιαστηρίου σου
verses.text_reference: 'to quench the glory of your house and your altar, to open the mouths of the nations for the praise of vain idols.'
verses.rendering: to extinguish the glory of your house and your altar, and to open the mouths of the nations for the praise of worthless idols.'

verses.text_source: Greek: μὴ παραδῷς κύριε τὸ σκῆπτρόν σου τοῖς μὴ οὖσιν
verses.text_reference: 'O Lord, do not surrender your scepter to what has no being; do not let them laugh at our downfall, but turn their plan against them.'
verses.rendering: 'O Lord, do not surrender your scepter to beings that do not exist. Do not let them mock our ruin, but turn their plot against them,
translator_notes: 'Beings that do not exist' (tois mē ousin) — the idols are ontologically void. This is a philosophical argument against idol worship characteristic of Hellenistic Jewish thought (cf. Isaiah 44:9, Wisdom of Solomon 13-15).

verses.text_source: Greek: ἐπίβλεψον ἐφ᾽ ἡμᾶς κύριε
verses.text_reference: 'and make an example of him who began this against us. Remember, O Lord; make yourself known in this time of our affliction. Give me courage, O King of the gods and Master of all dominion.'
verses.rendering: and make an example of the one who began this evil against us. Remember us, O Lord! Make yourself known in the time of our affliction. Give me courage, O King of the gods and Master of every dominion.'
translator_notes: Esther's closing plea — 'give me courage' — directly anticipates her dangerous approach to the king in Addition D. The title 'King of the gods' (basileu tōn theōn) may strike a modern reader as polytheistic, but in Hellenistic Jewish usage it asserts God's supremacy over all supposed deities.
